coredata.dc:title: Fluorinated graphene intercalated by carbon quantum dots: Triple effect for reducing friction and wear
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ding
Jia, Xiaohua
Yang, Jin
Wang, Zhaofeng
Song, Haojie
coredata.dc:description: Traditional graphene-based lubrication materials, especially in long-term running conditions, are often face with problems such as reduced lubrication performance and serious wear, which limits their further applications. In this study, a special intercalation structure (FG/rCQDs) was synthesized through confining the carbon quantum dots to the interlayer of multilayer fluorinated graphene benefiting from the weak interlayer force of FG layers and small size of CQDs, respectively. Moreover, structural and chemical changes of this intercalation structure can be induced by friction heat and shear force generated in the process of friction. By analyzing the tribological behavior in different stages, we find that triple effects (rolling effect, filling effect, tribo-film effect) can be triggered in different lubrication stages, thus greatly extending the lubrication life. Especially, CQDs being used as interlayer bearings and repair wear tracks play a crucial role in the triple effect. Ultimately, the triple effect generated by FG/rCQDs intercalation structure formed by carbon quantum dots and fluorinated graphene layers can synergistically work to significantly reduce friction and wear. The study shows that this special intercalation structure can show great potential in the field of lubrication.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Intercalation structure
Fluorinated graphene
Carbon quantum dots
Triple effect

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52473334); The Youth Innovation Team of Shaanxi Universities (24JP027); Key Research and Development Plan of Shaanxi Province (2023-YBGY-429); and Open Fund of Shandong Laboratory for Yantai Advanced Materials and Green Manufacturing (AMGM2024F06).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustrations of FG/rCQDs. (b) FTIR spectrum of bulk FGi, CQDs and FG/rCQDs. (c) Raman spectrum of bulk FGi, CQDs and FG/rCQDs. (d) XRD spectrum of bulk FGi, CQDs and FG/rCQDs. (e) TEM images of CQDs. (f) Lateral size distribution of CQDs. (g-j) HRTEM images of FGi and FG/rCQDs.

(a) SEM image of FGi. (b, c) TEM images of and FG/CQDs. (d-i) XPS survey spectra of stacked FGi and FG/rCQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of F 1s (e) and C 1s (f) of stacked FGi, C 1s (g), O 1s (h) and F 1s (i) of FG/rCQDs.

(a) Friction curve, (b) average friction coefficient and (c) wear track width and contact pressure at 0.1–1.0 wt% concentration. (d) Friction curves and (e) average friction coefficient and (f) wear track width and contact pressure under different loads (1–4 N). (g) Friction curve, (h) average friction coefficient and (i) wear track width and contact pressure at 100–400 rpm.

(a) Long-term friction curve lubricated by Mechanical mixing between FGi and CQDs, pure PAO and FG/CQDs. (b) Raman spectrum of FG/rCQDs before and after friction. (c) Tribological mechanism diagram at one stage (rolling effect). (d) Friction curve lubricated by mechanical mixing between FGi and CQDs, pure PAO, FG/CQDs, FG and CQDs.

(a) Long-term friction curve lubricated by mechanical mixing between FGi and CQDs, pure PAO and FG/CQDs, wear changes at second stage lubricated by pure PAO and tribo-film pattern at third stage (tribo-film effect). (b) Wear surface and three-dimensional morphology after optical microscope photo processing at second stage. (c) Tribological mechanism diagram at second stage (filling effect). (d) SEM images and Element mappings images of the worn surface.

XPS spectra of worn surfaces of 0.25 wt% FG/rCQDs: (a) C 1s fine spectra, (b) O 1s fine spectra and (c) F 3d fine spectra. (d-e) Raman spectrum with different friction time. (f-i) Optical microscope analysis and three-dimensional morphology of wear surface with and without 0.25 wt% FG/rCQDs.

(a-d) Roughness of steel disc and ball wear surfaces. (e) The schematic of transfer mechanism of CQDs on the wear surface. (f) The schematic of lubrication mechanisms under FG/rCQDs during the friction process.

head.ce:title: Fluorinated graphene intercalated by carbon quantum dots: Triple effect for reducing friction and wear

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Green Preparation and Functionalization for Inorganic Materials, Shaanxi University of Science & Technology, Xi’an 710021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Green Preparation and Functionalization for Inorganic Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Green Preparation and Functionalization for Inorganic Materials, Shaanxi University of Science & Technology, Xi’an 710021, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Laboratory for Yantai Advanced Materials and Green Manufacturing, Yantai 264006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Laboratory for Yantai Advanced Materials and Green Manufacturing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Laboratory for Yantai Advanced Materials and Green Manufacturing, Yantai 264006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Traditional graphene-based lubrication materials, especially in long-term running conditions, are often face with problems such as reduced lubrication performance and serious wear, which limits their further applications. In this study, a special intercalation structure (FG/rCQDs) was synthesized through confining the carbon quantum dots to the interlayer of multilayer fluorinated graphene benefiting from the weak interlayer force of FG layers and small size of CQDs, respectively. Moreover, structural and chemical changes of this intercalation structure can be induced by friction heat and shear force generated in the process of friction. By analyzing the tribological behavior in different stages, we find that triple effects (rolling effect, filling effect, tribo-film effect) can be triggered in different lubrication stages, thus greatly extending the lubrication life. Especially, CQDs being used as interlayer bearings and repair wear tracks play a crucial role in the triple effect. Ultimately, the triple effect generated by FG/rCQDs intercalation structure formed by carbon quantum dots and fluorinated graphene layers can synergistically work to significantly reduce friction and wear. The study shows that this special intercalation structure can show great potential in the field of lubrication.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous progress of industrial technology, the performance requirements of lubricating materials are becoming higher and higher [1–3]. Especially under long-term operation conditions, traditional graphene-based lubricating materials often face the problems of poor lubrication performance and serious wear, restricting their further application in the field of lubrication [4,5]. It can be attributed to the poor dispersion stability and insufficient antioxidant properties [6–8]. First, the layered structure of graphene easily restacks during repeated friction processes, reducing its effective contact area, thereby reducing the anti-wear and anti-friction effect [9,10]. Second, In the actual use of graphene additives, there are also significant adverse effects such as composite oxides and soot in engine lubricants, which limits its potential in improving the oxidation resistance of lubricating oils [11]. Therefore, the durability and reliability of lubricating materials required by continuously operating mechanical components cannot be met.

To effectively enhance the lubrication performance of materials and prolong their lubrication life, researchers have begun to explore new lubrication materials. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a novel type of carbon-based material, offer numerous advantages, including small size, spherical morphology, low cost, and environmental friendliness [12–15]. The preparation methods of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are mainly divided into two categories: “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches. The top-down method primarily involves cutting or exfoliating small-sized CQDs from large carbon materials through physical or chemical means. However, this method requires sophisticated equipment and yields a low production rate. In contrast, the bottom-up method synthesizes CQDs by polymerizing small-molecule precursors through chemical reactions. This approach is characterized by its simplicity, low cost, and suitability for large-scale production. Thanks to their unique preparation methods and excellent properties, CQDs have found widespread applications in the fields of biomedicine, optoelectronics, and sensors. Their small size enables CQDs to easily enter the wear gap, form a protective film, and reduce wear. Moreover, Due to its rich functional groups and special electronic structure, CQDs can be used as an effective antioxidant in different applications, making CQDs show great potential in the field of anti-oxidation of lubricants. Therefore, CQDs have received widespread attention in the field of lubrication. The significant friction-reduction and anti-wear performance can be achieved via nano-bearing and nano-filling mechanisms of minimized CDs sediment particles (deposited in situ on worn a-C surfaces) [16]. The nano-bearing mechanism lies in utilizing the unique structures and size effects of nanomaterials to make the relative motion between contact surfaces smoother, while the nano-filling mechanism refers to the process where nanoparticles fill in the tiny pits or defects on the surface during friction, thereby improving surface roughness and reducing frictional resistance. Moreover, NCDs prepared by a two-step hydrothermal method can strongly adsorb onto carbon steel surfaces. During friction, they migrate via thermal motion, adsorb parallelly, and donate π-electrons to Fe atoms at the interface. Once a sufficient amount of NCDs is adsorbed, they form a protective film on the carbon steel surface, which functions as an effective barrier to protect the friction interface [17]. It is well documented that CQDs modified by ionic liquid demonstrates high lubricity in triethanolamine aqueous solution [18,19]. Because of its special structure and properties, CQDs has a huge application prospect in the field of lubrication.

Fluorinated graphene (FG), as an emerging graphene derivative, enjoys the wider layer spacing of around 0.5 nm and weaker layer interaction due to the introduction of fluorine [20–22]. There are several methods for preparing fluorinated graphene. First, direct fluorination of graphene or graphene oxide, although effective, demands stringent requirements for equipment and reaction conditions. Second, exfoliation of fluorinated graphite, while feasible, results in a low production rate and a complex separation process. Moreover, the FG not only inherits the excellent mechanical properties, but also induces relevant friction chemical reaction on the friction surface [23]. Chen et al. Prepared FG nanosheets by liquid-phase exfoliation and investigated the tribological performance. It can be concluded that the wear rate and friction coefficient containing 1 wt% FG at 10 N can be reduced by 87 % and 12.3 % compared with pure PAO, respectively [24]. Therefore, it can be regarded as a solid lubricant or lubricating additive.

Inspired by the intercalation structure of graphene quantum dots GQDs within MoS2 layers, which achieves high rate capability and ultra-long cycling life [25], we propose applying similar structures in friction. First, the intercalation of small size materials between layers can significantly avoid agglomeration and stacking of two-dimensional materials. Second, the surface functional groups of CQDs can significantly improve the oxidation resistance of lubricating materials. CQDs are defined as having diameters less than 10 nm and FG has wide layer spacing and weak interlayer force [26–29], which make them become ideal candidates for intercalation structure [30–35]. It will be expected to raise a novel tribological mechanism by combining the excellent properties of two materials [36]. These characteristics endow the FG/rCQDs composite lubrication materials with promising application prospects. They can provide a continuous and stable lubrication effect, thereby reducing maintenance costs and extending equipment life, especially under long-term operating conditions such as in high-speed bearings, gearboxes, and engines. Moreover, the low viscosity of PAO4 oil used in this study can shift lubrication from full-film to boundary conditions, causing severe surface contact and wear. This highlights the need for enhanced anti-wear and anti-friction properties. One effective solution is to use nano-additives in lubricants, which can reduce energy losses and pollutant emissions while protecting the environment.

Herein, we successfully prepare an intercalation structure and explain its outstanding anti-friction and anti-wear effect. Through confining the CQDs to the interlayer of FG nanosheets, a novel FG/reduced carbon quantum dots (FG/rCQDs) hybrid enjoying an ultra-large interlayer spacing (1.08 nm, expanded from 0.42 nm for the pristine FGi) and good lubricating property was prepared via an electrostatic self-assembly method. Even more impressively, induced by friction and friction heat, the tailored FG/rCQDs hybrid exhibit tribo-effect (rolling effect, filling effect, tribo-film effect) at different friction stages, which work synergistically to achieving excellent and durable lubricating performance. Therefore, the FG/rCQDs present a good practicality used as lubricating additive.
Details of Materials and reagents are described in Supporting Information, Text S1.

At room temperature, 20 mL of acetaldehyde was stirred in the beaker and 6 g of sodium hydroxide was added slowly. After 2 h, the product was ultrasonically treated several times with dilute hydrochloric acid and distilled water until the value of the pH was 7. The final product was dried in a freeze-dryer for 48 h. The principle of this method is that sodium hydroxide reacts with acetaldehyde to produce condensation and disproportionation reactions. The resulting oligomers contain many functional groups and branched chains. In the exothermic reaction, the branched chain was growing, wrapping together, and crossing-linked to form CQDs.

Then, 0.8 g hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) and 0.2 g FGi was dissolved in 30 mL DI water, followed by ball milling with 400 rpm for 8 h in the ball mill machine. where the electropositive CTA+ would absorb onto the electronegative FG layers through electrostatic attraction during continuous ultrasonication. CQDs solution (1.6 g CQDs dispersed in 20 mL DI water) was further infused into the exfoliated FG-CTA+ dispersion. The mixed liquid was sonicated for another 2 h to ensure the adsorption of the electronegative CQDs onto the electropositive FG-CTA+ surface through electrostatic interactions. Subsequently, the mixture was put into a flask with the addition of reducing agent (0.4 g L-ascorbic acid, C6H8O6). After the heating process (100°C for 8 h), CQDs was reduced to rCQDs, and the exfoliated FG layers would turbostratically restack with rCQDs sandwiched in interlayers. Ultimately, the target FG/rCQDs hybrid was obtained upon cleaning and freeze-drying.

The FG/rCQDs was placed in a 20 mL transparent glass reagent bottle. And an appropriate amount of PAO oil was added to configure lubricant dispersions with different mass fractions. Followed by stirring for 30 min and treating in a water bath with ultrasonication for 30 min to obtain uniform dispersions. As shown in Fig. S2, the obtained FG/rCQDs/PAO dispersion exhibits excellent dispersion stability after a day or even two days of placement. Friction tests were carried out under different load and speed conditions.
Details of Characterization and tribological performance evaluation are described in Supporting Information, Text S1.

The morphological characteristics of bulk FGi and FG/rCQDs are examined by the SEM and TEM analysis. The bulk FGi before intercalation exhibits a multilayered structure as shown in Fig. 2a, which provides a prerequisite for the insertion of CQDs [39]. After the intercalation process, as shown in Fig. 2b-c,the CQDs are uniformly distributed on the surface of the FG nanosheets, exhibiting the corresponding lattice fringe of CQDs. And the relevant parameter was analyzed and calculated in the supporting information. To sum up, the CQDs was intercalated between the FG nanolayer. The surface chemistry of stack FGi, FG/rCQDs was investigated by XPS, as shown in Fig. 2d-i. the survey spectra (Fig. 2d) indicate that Stack FGi and as prepared FG/rCQDs all contain C, O and F elements. High-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 2f) of stack FGi are displayed in further detail, and the peaks at 279.4, 281.2, 284.6, and 286.5 eV correspond to C-C, C=C, C-F, and C-F2, respectively. And the peaks of high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 2g) of FG/rCQDs at 279.3, 280.6, 284.5, 285.4, and 289.5 eV correspond to C-C/C-O, C=C, C-F, C-F2, and C-CF respectively. It can be concluded that preparation process can introduce oxygen into FG layers. And it can be proved by the high-resolution O 1s of FG/rCQDs, which demonstrates the successful preparation of FG/rCQDs (Fig. 2h). In addition, the high-resolution F 1s of stack FGi are displayed as shown in Fig. 2i, the peaks at 686.5 and 687.8 eV correspond to C-F and C-F2. And the peak of the high-resolution F 1s at 690.4 eV corresponding to the C-CF appears, which can be attributed to the edge defect caused by the preparation process. However, the fluorine content decreased slightly from 44.4 at% to 39.7 at%, which demonstrates that the intercalation of CQDs has no obvious effect on fluorine element.

The tribological behaviors of FG/rCQDs composite was examined by dispersing in PAO4 oil using rotary friction experiment and wear testing. Three significant factors (concentration, load and rotate speed) were used to tune the working conditions that are suitable for the FG/rCQDs composite.

The amounts of lubricating additives will affect its tribological behaviors, therefore, the tribological properties under different concentration of FG/rCQDs was examined as shown in Fig. 3 (a-b). It can be concluded that the average friction coefficient first decreased and then increased as the concentration increasing from 0.10 wt% to 1.0 wt%. And coefficient of friction reaches the lowest level (0.03) when the concentration is 0.25 wt%. when the concentration is too low, a small amount of intercalation structure fails to play various effects, so as to form a stable protect film. However, too much additive acting as an abrasive will fill the wear mark, causing damage to the friction interface. Therefore, the 0.25 wt% of concentration will be a suitable value. Furthermore, wear track width and calculated contact pressure was used to further explore the tribological behaviors on the friction surface. We found that the smaller wear track width is often able to withstand the bigger pressure. The applied load and rotate speed in the friction process are also closely related to the tribological properties. As shown in Fig. 3(d-f), the influence of load on tribological properties is conducted under different loads varied from 1 N to 4 N. In Fig. 3d, when the load is 1 N, the friction coefficient always maintains the relative low level in the initial stages of friction, but the curves suddenly rise at 15 min. Such phenomenon demonstrated that friction failure occurred in the process of friction, which can be attributed to the tribo-film was destroyed. In conclusion, the friction curve can maintain the lowest and stable level, and the coefficient of friction and wear track width can also reach the lowest value at 2 N. Meanwhile, the calculated contact pressure between the friction pair also achieves the maximum (437.8 MPa) when the concentration and load are 0.25 wt% and 2N, which can demonstrate that the FG/rCQDs under these conditions can bear higher contact pressure, and easily enter the friction interval, thereby realizing the favorable anti-wear and friction-reduction effect. During the friction process, the shear force will vary with the changing of the rotate speed, a suitable shear force can be good at lubricant process. There, the condition of rotate speed was determined to explore the influences of rotate speed on friction behaviors. In conclusion, the minimum coefficient was obtained under 200 rpm of rotate speed. And the lowest value of friction coefficient (0.03) and wear track width (53.9 μm) was obtained (Fig. 3g-h), compared with pure PAO4 oil, which was decreased by 79.9 % and 54.5 %.

To further study the tribological behaviors at different friction stages, as shown in Fig. 4 d, we conduct friction test by comparing different components among pure PAO, Mechanical mixing, FG, CQDs, and FG/rCQDs. In the 30 min of the friction test (Fig. 4d), the friction coefficient lubricated by the FG/rCQDs is significantly lower than that of other components. Compared with the mechanical mixing between FG and CQDs, the friction coefficient decreased from 0.11 to 0.03. And under the conditions of long-term friction, it can still maintain a stable state of continuous low friction, which demonstrates the unique advantage of this intercalation structure. To explore the detailed tribological mechanism on this stage, the long-term friction test (180 min) was conducted. As shown in Fig. 4b, Raman spectra around the first 60 min was examined to analyze the structural changes of this intercalation structure. It can be concluded that, before and after friction, the intensity of G peak locating at 1590 cm−1 has a remarkable reduction in the friction process. Moreover, the G’ peak also become few and weak, which can be attributed to the decreasing in the number of layers. In conclusion, In the process of friction, under the action of friction shear force, the FG/rCQDs forms a lubricating part similar to the “plate trailer”, converting the sliding friction into rolling friction between FG layers, thus playing a good friction-reduction effect (Fig. 4c).

To further demonstrate the process of material deconstruction, scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Fig. 5 b) images display that a relatively uniform layered fold structure of FG nanosheets appears at the wear tracks, which can be attributed to the shear force generated between the friction pairs and demonstrate the formation of the “plate trailer” structure. And the EDS mapping images exhibit that carbon and fluorine cover the surface of the abrasion. In conclusion, it can be concluded that the intercalation structure can be separated into the “plate trailer” structure on the surface of the abrasion, thus acting as a protective film, which is preliminary confirmation of the rolling effect that we mentioned.

In this process, the CQDs act as rolling bearings between the different FG layers, thus significantly reducing the coefficient of friction. Compared with the mechanical mixing of the two components, this intercalation structure has a lower friction coefficient, indicating that the rolling effect is unique to this intercalation structure.

As the friction test goes on, the wear surface was examined to further explore the micro-tribological behavior on the friction interface. The width and depth of the wear tracks are obvious at the initial stage lubricated by pure PAO oil, then it continues to deteriorate with 60 min of continuous friction in Fig. 5a. Although the wear track width increases from 60.1 μm to 95.4 μm, the wear track depth has no obvious deterioration (Fig. 5c). Therefore, it will be concluded that the CQDs will partially fall off from the intercalation structure under the action of continuous shear force. Due to the small size effect of CQDs, when the friction interface appears very small nanoscale wear gaps, it can easily enter the wear track to plug the gap, preventing further deterioration of the wear track from the shear force. This stage can be summarized as the “filling effect” to work effectively for anti-wear (Fig. 5d).

The heat and pressure generated in the friction process are easy to cause friction chemical reactions at the worn surface. To verify the tribo-film effect, the chemical component on the friction surface was examined using the XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the presence of C 1s characteristic peaks in the spectra of the wear surface lubricated with FG/rCQDs reflected the existence of the FG layers during the friction process. Due to the excellent lubricating properties and mechanical stability of FG nanosheets, a stable and hard film can be formed between the friction interface. Moreover, the fine spectra of O 1s can indicate the existence of CQDs, which also proves the filling effect of CQDs at the wear track [40–42]. In the fine spectra of F 1s (Fig. 6c), the characteristic peaks at 686.1 eV can be ascribed to the metallic fluorides forming on the surface (possibly FeF2 or FeF3). It can be demonstrated that relevant friction chemical reactions induced by shear force and friction heat could occur on the wear surface, leading to the formation of fluoride, and adsorbing on the surface of friction to form the one-layer adsorption film, as shown in Fig. 6c. Furthermore, Raman analysis was used to explore the structural and chemical changes at the end of the friction as shown in Fig. 6d, e. The obvious peak at around 600 cm−1 is ascribed to the formation of iron compound (FeFx). The value of ID/IG increased from 0.86 to 0.97, which reflects the increase of defect level for FG layers, explaining that the FG nanosheets can be used as a protective film to bear the damage of shear force under friction. The double tribo-film formed by adsorption film of metallic fluorides and FG layers can work synergistically to protect the friction pair from the abrasive dust as shown in Fig. 6a. In the end, we compare the wear track width between pure PAO and FG/rCQDs, compared with the pure PAO, the wear track width can be reduced from 118.4 μm to 53.9 μm, the wear track width exhibiting significant reduction, which demonstrates the favorable anti-wear effect (Fig. 6f-i).

Based on the above discussion, the lubricant performances of PAO4 can be significantly enhanced by adding FG/rCQDs into PAO oil, thereby the lubrication state needs to further calculate. The lubrication state is determined by the ratio of the minimum oil film thickness (h0) to the roughness at the wear point (σ) [43]. The calculation formula is below:
(1)
h 0
= 2.69
G ∗
0.53
U
∗
0.67
R
W
∗
0.067
1 - 0.61
e
- 0.73 k
where W*=W/E1R2, G*=αE1, U*=ηu/E1R, and α and η are the pressure-viscosity coefficient and the dynamic viscosity of the lubricant (α = 22 GPa−1, η = 5.7 × 10-2 Pa·s) respectively, and u is the velocity of the friction pair (62.8 mm/s); The sphere radius is expressed as R (2 mm); W1 is the load during the experiment (1 N); k is the coefficient (about 1.0); E is the effective Young’s modulus of the friction pair, which can be calculated by the following equation:
(2)
1 E
=
1 2
1 -
v 1 2
E 1
+
1 -
v 2 2
E 2
The above vi and Ei are the Poisson’s ratio and elasticity modulus of the friction material, respectively. (v1 = 0.269, v2 = 0.3; E1 = 209 GPa, E2 = 219 GPa). The calculated E is 233 GPa.

The calculated film thickness (h0) is 31.6 nm. Moreover, the surface roughness of friction pair is tested using Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) analysis, therefore, the lubrication state can be determined by these two main parameters [44]. The specific calculation process is in the following formula:
(3)
λ =
h 0
σ 1 2
+
σ 2 2
σ1 (4.29 nm) and σ2 (2.04 nm) in above formula are the surface roughness of the steel discs and balls in the friction area, respectively (Fig. 7 a, b). Ultimately, the λ value calculated is 6.6 nm, demonstrating the lubrication state is hydrodynamic lubrication. That is, lubricant can fill the friction gap, resulting in no contact between the two friction pairs under the hydrodynamic status, thereby reducing observably the friction coefficient and wear rate.

In conclusion, in the initial stage of friction, under the action of frictional shear force, the FG/rCQDs of intercalation structure forms a lubricating part similar to the “ plate trailer”, which can convert the sliding friction into rolling friction due to the CQDs between layers, and significantly reduce the coefficient of friction. With the progress of friction, small wear tracks are generated on the friction interface. Under the action of shear force, the CQDs on the surface of the FG/rCQDs will fall off, thus generating a number of free CQDs in the friction gap. Since the CQDs are negative charged, under the dua action of thermal motion and electric charge attraction, the free CQDs will be attracted to positively charged wear tracks and plug the wear tracks (Fig. 7e), thus playing a filling effect on the wear tracks. With the deepening of the friction process, the defluorination will occur on the FG nanosheets with the action of shear force and friction heat, thus producing lots of free fluoride ions. In the meanwhile, a large number of positive charges were generated between the friction pair, the fluoride ions will be attracted into the friction pair to form an adsorbed layer composed of iron fluoride. The fluorine has strong electronegativity [45], which can combine easily with iron metal ions, thereby forming iron fluoride. Compared with iron oxide, iron fluoride has advantages of being more stable, thus playing more everlasting protective film effect between the friction pairs [46], which plays an essential part in anti-wear and anti-friction [47]. The above analysis can be illustrated in Fig. 7f [48,49].

In summary, we propose, for the first time, an intercalation structure in the field of lubrication and reveal its excellent properties. The electrostatic self-assembly strategy was employed to prepare a novel FG/rCQDs hybrid, which enjoys an ultra-large interlayer distance of 1.08 nm, weak interlayer force and reinforced layered structure. When used as a lubricant additive for lubricating oil, the FG/rCQDs exhibits excellent tribological properties. And different effects can be triggered under different friction stages. Triple effect (rolling effect, filling effect, tribo-film effect) work synergistically to realizing excellent friction-reduction and anti-wear effect. The friction coefficient and wear track width can be decreased by 79.9 % and 54.5 %, respectively. Meanwhile, the FG/rCQDs dispersed in the PAO oil can still maintain a stable and effective lubrication state under long-term friction experiments (180 min), exhibiting great prospects in practical applications. This work unveils the concealed “intercalation” mechanism in the field of lubrication and offers new perspectives to design high-performance lubricant additives.

Ding Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Xiaohua Jia: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Jin Yang: Software. Zhaofeng Wang: Formal analysis. Haojie Song: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52473334); The Youth Innovation Team of Shaanxi Universities (24JP027); Key Research and Development Plan of Shaanxi Province (2023-YBGY-429); and Open Fund of Shandong Laboratory for Yantai Advanced Materials and Green Manufacturing (AMGM2024F06).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2025.162393.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
